---
title: "Chapter 1: Introduction"
subtitle: "STA258 — University of Toronto Mississauga"
---

<style>
.lecture-actions {
  display: flex;
  gap: 12px;
  margin: 24px 0 36px 0;
}
.lecture-actions .btn {
  font-size: 1em;
  padding: 10px 22px;
  border-radius: 8px;
}
.lecture-actions .btn-dark {
  background: #00695c;
  border-color: #00695c;
}
.lecture-actions .btn-dark:hover {
  background: #00574c;
  border-color: #00574c;
}
.summary-card {
  background: #f7f9fa;
  border-left: 6px solid #00695c;
  padding: 18px 22px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  margin: 18px 0;
}
.summary-card h3 {
  margin-top: 0;
  color: #00695c;
}
</style>

<div class="lecture-actions">
<a href="chapter1-slides.html" class="btn btn-outline-secondary" target="_blank">Slides</a>
<a href="chapter1-slides.html?print-pdf" class="btn btn-outline-secondary" target="_blank">PDF</a>
</div>

## What you'll learn

By the end of Chapter 1 you'll be able to:

- Distinguish a **population** from a **sample**, and identify the unit, observation, and variables in a dataset.
- Tell a **parameter** apart from a **statistic** — and know which is usually known.
- Classify data as **quantitative** (discrete or continuous) or **qualitative** (nominal or ordinal).
- Recognize common **sampling methods** and the **biases** they introduce.
- Explain the goal of **statistical inference**: using a sample to learn about a population, with a measure of uncertainty.

## Key ideas

::: {.summary-card}
### Statistics in one sentence
The science of learning from data **under uncertainty** — moving from what we measured to what we want to know.
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Population vs Sample
A **population** is the full group we want to learn about. A **sample** is the subset we actually measure. We use the sample to make claims about the population.
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Parameter vs Statistic
A **parameter** (e.g. $\mu$, $p$, $\sigma$) summarizes the population — usually unknown. A **statistic** (e.g. $\bar{x}$, $\hat{p}$, $s$) summarizes the sample — known, and used to estimate the parameter.
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Types of Data
**Quantitative** data are numeric: discrete (counts) or continuous (measured).
**Qualitative** data are categorical: nominal (no order) or ordinal (ordered).
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Inference
We collect a sample, calculate a statistic, and use it to estimate an unknown population parameter — always with a measure of uncertainty. This sets up confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and the rest of the course.
:::

## How to use this chapter

- Open the **slides** above for the full walkthrough, with interactive R code blocks you can edit and run in your browser.
- Use the **PDF** button for an offline-friendly version (interactive elements won't run there).
- Try the practice exercises from the textbook at the end of Chapter 1.
